2603.26062.title: I Want to Believe (but the Vocabulary Changed): Measuring the Semantic Structure and Evolution of Conspiracy Theories
2603.26062.paper_summary: Research on conspiracy theories has largely focused on belief formation, exposure, and diffusion, while paying less attention to how their meanings change over time. This gap persists partly because conspiracy-related terms are often treated as stable lexical markers, making it difficult to separate genuine semantic changes from surface-level vocabulary changes. In this paper, we measure the semantic structure and evolution of conspiracy theories in online political discourse. Using 169.9M comments from Reddit's r/politics subreddit spanning 2012--2022, we first demonstrate that conspiracy-related language forms coherent and semantically distinguishable regions of language space, allowing conspiracy theories to be treated as semantic objects. We then track how these objects evolve over time using aligned word embeddings, enabling comparisons of semantic neighborhoods across periods. Our analysis reveals that conspiracy theories evolve non-uniformly, exhibiting patterns of semantic stability, expansion, contraction, and replacement that are not captured by keyword-based approaches alone.
2603.26062.paper_summary_zh: 研究陰謀論的研究主要集中在信念形成、曝光和擴散上，而較少關注其意義隨時間的變化。這一空白部分存在的原因之一是與陰謀相關的術語通常被視為穩定的詞彙標記，使得將真正的語義變化與表面詞彙變化區分開來變得困難。在本文中，我們測量了在線政治話語中陰謀論的語義結構和演變。利用2012年至2022年間來自Reddit的r/politics子版塊的1.699億條評論，我們首先證明了與陰謀相關的語言形成了語言空間中一致且語義上可區分的區域，允許將陰謀論視為語義對象。然後，我們使用對齊的詞嵌入追蹤這些對象隨時間的演變，使得可以跨時期比較語義鄰域。我們的分析顯示，陰謀論的演變並不均勻，展現出語義穩定性、擴展、收縮和替代的模式，而這些模式僅通過基於關鍵詞的方法無法捕捉。
2603.26062.author: Manisha Keim et.al.
2603.26062.authors: Manisha Keim, Sarmad Chandio, Osama Khalid, Rishab Nithyanand
